title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Impact of Fast Food on Local Food Cultures

texts.A: [37]The relentless global spread of multinational fast-food chains represents a particularly insidious form of 'culinary colonialism' that is systematically erasing the extraordinary flavor, diversity, and irreplaceable heritage of our planet's food cultures.[/37] When every major city across the world offers the same standardized burger, the same franchise fried chicken, and the same globally sourced ingredients, we irreversibly lose the unique and precious culinary traditions that communities have carefully cultivated and passed down through generations. [38]This loss is not merely about food as sustenance; it is about the profound destruction of the broader social structures—the ritual of the family meal, the communal life of the local market, the cultural identity embedded in a regional recipe—that these living food traditions have always supported.[/38] [40]The cold industrial efficiency of the assembly-line kitchen, however impressive as a logistical feat, is no adequate substitute for the soul, creativity, and human story of a genuine local kitchen.[/40]
texts.B: The widely stated 'threat' of multinational fast food to authentic local culture is, upon closer examination, largely an oversimplified myth perpetuated by a certain class of privileged food critics. In practice and in most countries around the world, international chains have been obliged to substantially adapt and localize their menus to accommodate specific regional tastes and dietary preferences, in the process creating genuinely innovative hybrid cuisines that reflect real cultural exchange. [39]Furthermore, these globally standardized chains consistently provide safe, reliably affordable, and hygienically consistent food options that are particularly valuable for the millions of people who are constantly on the move in rapidly urbanizing societies.[/39] Fast food is not replacing traditional home cooking; it is simply providing a convenient and accessible alternative for a busy, time-pressed, and increasingly urbanized global population with genuine needs. [37]Local food cultures have proven themselves to be remarkably resilient and are perfectly capable of coexisting alongside global brands.[/37]
texts.C: I agree that the much-discussed homogenization of global tastes and culinary habits is a valid and genuinely important cultural concern that deserves serious and sustained policy attention, but I would argue that the primary and more fundamental blame lies more squarely with the broader industrialization of global agriculture and the complex global food supply chain than with any specific restaurant chain or international food brand. [38]I share the view that standardized fast food has substantially contributed to the gradual erosion of our shared social fabric around mealtimes, but I believe this is fundamentally a symptom of a relentlessly faster-paced and more atomized modern lifestyle, rather than its root cause or primary driver.[/38] We should therefore focus our collective energy and serious policy efforts on comprehensive food education—teaching children and adults alike the genuine cultural, nutritional, and environmental value of local seasonal ingredients and the rich history of their own regional cuisine—thereby empowering them to make genuinely informed and conscious choices, rather than resorting to the blunt instrument of simply banning specific types of food businesses from operating.
texts.D: The remarkable commercial success of fast-food giants across every continent is ultimately an honest testament to the fundamental power of a genuinely free market responding to real consumer demand. People consistently choose these options because they offer demonstrably better perceived value, predictable quality, and reliable convenience than many traditional alternatives, which are frequently inaccessible or unaffordable. While I share a degree of nostalgic appreciation for the atmosphere and authenticity of traditional local markets and family-run establishments, we must honestly acknowledge that such establishments were historically often inaccessible and unaffordable for ordinary working-class families. [39]Fast food has, in a very real and meaningful sense, genuinely 'democratized' the experience of eating out and accessing affordable protein.[/39] [40]The legitimate and pressing issue is the well-documented public health impact—the dangerously high levels of processed salt, refined sugar, and saturated fat—not the abstract 'cultural' impact that cultural critics fixate upon.[/40] We should regulate ingredients rigorously, not police brand presence.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the resilience of local food traditions in the face of fast-food expansion?
questions.explanation: Expert B believes local cultures are 'resilient' and can 'coexist.' Expert A argues that fast food is 'erasing the flavor and diversity' and replacing unique traditions.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's concern about the loss of social structures associated with traditional dining?
questions.explanation: Expert A laments the loss of the 'family meal' and 'social structures.' Expert C agrees that 'fast food has damaged our social fabric.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to D regarding the economic accessibility (democratization) of fast food?
questions.explanation: Expert D says fast food has 'democratized' eating out and offers 'better value.' Expert B also mentions it provides 'affordable' options for people on the move.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from D on what the 'real issue' with fast food is?
questions.explanation: Expert D argues the health impact is the real issue, not the cultural one. Expert A argues the cultural impact ('culinary colonialism') and the loss of diversity is the fundamental problem.
